title: The long game
full_text: 21st March, 2026 19 Mar 2026 | By Venessa Anthony 19 Mar 2026 | By Venessa Anthony On most mornings, before the Colombo heat fully settles in, Mithun Liyanage is already at work. Not behind a desk, but on the road, in the water, or pacing alongside an athlete who is learning, often the hard way, that endurance is less about brute strength and more about discipline, patience, and precision. It is this philosophy that sits at the heart of Endurance Lab, the training platform he founded to reshape how Sri Lankan athletes approach endurance sport. Building something that did not exist Endurance Lab was not born out of convenience. It emerged from a gap Mithun had experienced firsthand. Too many athletes, he observed, were training without structure, pushing themselves into cycles of injury and burnout. There was enthusiasm, even talent – but not enough guidance. “I started the Endurance Lab because I saw a lot of athletes making the same mistakes,” Mithun explained. “They were training hard, but not training smart, and that leads to burnout and injury.” He founded the platform with a clear intention: to create a space where athletes could train correctly. That meant structured programmes, attention to recovery, and a scientific approach to performance. It also meant challenging a mindset that often equated more effort with better results. “For me, it was about creating a system where athletes understand why they’re doing what they’re doing,” he stated. “It’s not just about following a plan – it’s about learning.” From the outset, Mithun positioned himself not just as a coach, but as an educator. His work extends beyond training plans; it is about teaching athletes how to listen to their bodies, how to pace themselves, and how to sustain performance over time. In a country where endurance sport is still carving out its identity, this approach has proven quietly transformative. Starting up in uncertain times If building a niche training platform was not challenging enough, timing added another layer of difficulty. Endurance Lab was launched just before the Easter Sunday attacks in 2019, a moment that shook Sri Lanka’s social and economic landscape. Not long after, the COVID-19 pandemic brought further disruption. The impact on the business was immediate and stark. A community that once included around 40 athletes shrank to just 10. Races were cancelled. Training routines were interrupted. For a model built around consistency, it was a severe test. “We went from having about 40 athletes to around 10 during COVID,” Mithun said. “It was a very difficult period, not just for the business but for the athletes as well.” Yet, rather than pause operations, Mithun adapted. He shifted focus to local events and reoriented training goals. Through his sister venture, The Fitness Formula, he expanded into general fitness, opening the door for a wider audience beyond competitive endurance athletes. “We had to rethink everything,” he explained. “There were no international races, so we focused on local rides and general fitness. It was about keeping people moving.” This period revealed a defining trait of his coaching style: adaptability. When international races were no longer an option, he encouraged athletes to find purpose in local rides, personal milestones, and the simple act of showing up. “You have to adapt,” he stated. “That’s what endurance is about – not just physically, but mentally as well.” Coaching beyond the physical What sets Mithun apart is the breadth of his role. Coaching, in his world, is not limited to physical training. It is psychological, logistical, and deeply personal. He speaks often about the importance of goals – not as distant achievements, but as anchors that keep athletes grounded when circumstances shift. “Goals are very important,” he explained. “Even if races are cancelled, you still need something to work towards. It keeps you consistent.” During the pandemic, when motivation dipped across the board, this focus on goal-setting helped sustain a sense of direction within the Endurance Lab community. His coaching also reflects a strong understanding of context. Training in Sri Lanka presents unique challenges, from weather conditions to infrastructure limitations. Rather than replicate global models wholesale, Mithun adapts them. “You can’t just take a programme from somewhere else and apply it here,” he said. “You have to consider the conditions, the lifestyle, everything.” Nutrition is another area where his influence is evident. He has been vocal about the need for endurance athletes in Sri Lanka to rethink dietary habits. “In Sri Lanka, we eat a lot of carbs, which is fine, but endurance athletes also need protein and fats,” he stated. “Recovery depends on it.” Through the Endurance Lab, he integrates nutritional awareness into training, ensuring athletes are supported holistically. Learning from the world stage Mithun’s own experience as an athlete informs much of his coaching philosophy. His qualification for the IRONMAN 70.3 World Championship in South Africa in 2018 remains a defining moment. Competing on a global stage exposed him to conditions far removed from those in Sri Lanka. “The water was freezing,” he recalled. “It was completely different from what we’re used to here.” It was a lesson in adaptability. Preparation, he realised, is not just about physical readiness but about anticipating and adjusting to the unknown. “You have to be ready for anything,” he explained. “Conditions can change, and you need to adjust quickly.” This insight now feeds directly into his coaching, where he emphasises flexibility alongside discipline. The story that defines a coach Among the many athletes Mithun has worked with, one story stands out for its depth and impact: that of Khaled Ashman, a visually impaired endurance athlete. Coaching Khaled required more than technical expertise. It demanded trust, coordination, and an entirely different approach to training. “With Khaled, everything is about communication and trust,” Mithun said. “When we’re in the water, we’re connected by a tether. On the bike, we’re on a tandem. We have to move as one.” During races, Mithun guided Khaled through swims using a tether, ensuring synchronisation in open water....

title: A clinic that grows with its patients
full_text: 21st March, 2026 19 Mar 2026 | By Venessa Anthony 19 Mar 2026 | By Venessa Anthony Healthcare can often feel hurried and impersonal, despite this, Vida Medical Clinic has steadily shaped a different narrative, one that places people, not processes, at the centre. Now, just a few years since its founding in 2021, the clinic is entering a new phase of growth with the opening of a larger, purpose-built facility in Colombo 5. This relocation is more than a change of address. It reflects an evolving understanding of what patients need today: not just treatment, but continuity, comfort, and care that adapts to their lives. Built on care, expanded with purpose From its early days, Vida Medical Clinic built its reputation on a simple but demanding standard – combining clinical expertise with a sense of empathy that patients could feel. As its patient base grew, so did the need for a space that could support more comprehensive services without compromising that ethos. For Founder and Managing Director Devin Ambalangodage, the decision to expand was both strategic and deeply personal. “The opening of our new clinic marks an important milestone for Vida Medical Clinic and reinforces our commitment to elevating the standard of care available to our patients,” he stated. “This investment enables us to provide more comprehensive services and a more comfortable clinical environment, ensuring patients receive the highest quality care in one convenient location.” The new facility, spanning over 4,000 square feet, has been designed with intention. Every detail – from wider spaces that support patient mobility to ergonomic workstations for medical professionals – points to a careful balancing act between efficiency and comfort. Designing a better patient experience Step inside the new clinic, and the difference is immediately noticeable. The layout is structured not just around medical functions, but around how patients actually move through a healthcare space. Three consultation rooms, a physiotherapy suite, a dedicated psychology consultation room, and a fully equipped procedure room allow for a range of services to operate simultaneously without crowding or delay. High-low examination tables, including bariatric-friendly equipment, ensure accessibility for patients with different physical needs. A spacious waiting lounge replaces the often-cramped waiting areas typical of urban clinics, creating an environment that feels calmer and more considered. It is a subtle shift, but one that acknowledges the emotional dimension of seeking care, something that is frequently overlooked. Perhaps one of the most practical additions is the in-house laboratory. “By enabling patients to complete essential diagnostic tests on-site, Vida reduces the need for travel between multiple facilities, an often frustrating aspect of healthcare in Colombo,” he added. Here, convenience is not treated as a luxury, but as part of care itself. Care that extends beyond the clinic While the physical expansion marks a significant step, Vida’s broader focus lies in extending care beyond its walls. The clinic has steadily developed its home healthcare services, offering doctor consultations at residences, workplaces, and hotels. This approach recognises a simple reality: not every patient can – or should – have to travel for care. “Whether it is elderly individuals, busy professionals, or international visitors, the ability to access reliable medical support where they are adds a layer of flexibility that traditional systems often lack,” he said. At the same time, Vida has strengthened its emergency response capabilities, operating 24-hour services supported by an islandwide ambulance network. “In medical emergencies, every second matters,” Ambalangodage noted. “Our dedicated ambulance service and trained emergency teams allow us to respond quickly and provide critical care when it is needed most, ensuring patients receive immediate and professional medical attention.” The emphasis here is not just speed, but preparedness, ensuring that patients receive stabilised, professional care even before reaching a hospital. Expanding services for modern health needs Healthcare needs are shifting, and Vida’s growing list of services reflects this change. Alongside its core offerings – family medicine, specialist consultations, physiotherapy, sports medicine, nutrition planning, sexual health services, and mental healthcare – the clinic is introducing newer treatments aimed at both recovery and prevention. Platelet-Rich Plasma (PRP) therapy, for instance, is now available to support wound healing, assist recovery from joint injuries, and aid in hair restoration. Similarly, multivitamin IV therapy has been introduced to address concerns such as fatigue, immunity, and overall wellness. Ambalangoda noted: “These additions point to a broader understanding of health – not just as the absence of illness, but as an ongoing state that requires attention, maintenance, and, at times, intervention.” Importantly, he also explained that these services are integrated within the same system, allowing patients to move between treatments without fragmentation. It is a model that prioritises continuity, reducing the gaps that often exist between different aspects of care. Prioritising dignity in elderly care Among Vida’s initiatives, Serenity by Vida stands out for its focus on elderly patients – a group whose needs are often complex and, at times, overlooked. Delivered directly to patients’ homes, the programme combines medical care with emotional and nutritional support, recognising that wellbeing in later life extends beyond clinical treatment. It is an approach that values dignity, independence, and comfort, while still ensuring that medical needs are met. In a context where families are increasingly balancing work and caregiving responsibilities, such services offer both practical support and reassurance. Technology as an enabler, not a barrier Another dimension of Vida’s growth lies in its use of technology. Through its digital platform, patient records are securely stored and can be accessed by authorised family members when needed. This system not only improves continuity of care but also introduces a level of transparency that empowers patients and their families. Medical histories, test results, and treatment plans are no longer confined to paper files or isolated consultations – they become part of an ongoing, accessible record. “In this sense, technology serves as a bridge rather than a barrier, connecting patients, families, and healthcare providers in a more cohesive way,” he explained. A vision grounded in accessibility What ties all these developments together is a...

title: Awards Season!
full_text: 21st March, 2026 18 Mar 2026 | By Apsara Rodrigo 18 Mar 2026 | By Apsara Rodrigo Many of us stay in line to watch our favorite movies in theaters. Almost all of us have thoughts about our favorite movies and shows. So when the 98th Academy Awards took place on the 15 March at the Dolby Theater, many of us tuned in to watch. “I was so eager to watch this year’s awards ceremony because I’ve been praying for the recognition of Frankenstein and for the downfall of Timothee Chalamet,” Rajni Tiwari, 17, said. Following the expression of so many opinions and emotions, The Daily Morning Brunch spoke to our readers on what they felt about this year’s Oscars. Fan favorites While many of us have different perspectives, lots of readers were impressed with the winners. “I’m so happy Michael B Jordan won,” Nethmi Amanda,26, said. “He deserves it.” Amanda also said that she is happy that Hamnet got the recognition it deserved. “ Hamnet will always have a special place in my heart because I’m a huge fan of historical retellings,” she said. Meanwhile, others pointed out the importance of recognising the music that makes a film successful. “Ludwig Goranson is a great musical composer,” Ashwin Ranasinghe, 22, said. “ Black panther, Oppenheimer and Sinners all had amazing scores and I’m glad that his work was recognised this year.” Beyond the mainstream winners, a number of readers highlighted the importance of representation and international stories. For some, the acknowledgment of films from the Global South carried as much weight as the major awards themselves. “I think The Voice of Hind Rajab making it to the Oscars is a big deal,”  Buddhini Rajawasam, 25, said. “I don’t think Hollywood has any useful say over politics, considering most of them are apolitical hypocrites and Zionists themselves but the fact that this was acknowledged means a lot for movies in the Global South, those who are facing illegal occupations and war, as a result of funding that's directed by the powers that be in the Global North, particularly America itself.” For Rajawasam and many others, this recognition represents a small step toward visibility for stories that are often ignored. While the global film industry remains dominated by North American studios, the inclusion of films like The Voice of Hind Rajab signals that the voices of marginalised regions are beginning to break through. Of course, not everyone was satisfied. For some, the night was marked by what was missing. “I really thought Emma Stone would win,” Kavindu Perera, 24, said. “Bugonia felt like such a defining film of the year and I expected her performance to be recognised.” Others felt that certain performances were overlooked in favor of safer choices. “There were a few wins that felt predictable,” said Dinuki Fernando, 21. “ While I definitely didn’t want Timothee Chalamet to win anything, I wanted at least one surprise that made me rethink the way I saw the nominees.” Yet even in disappointment, viewers found conversation and debate. For many, this is part of the Oscars’ enduring legacy. The ceremony itself Beyond the awards, audiences had plenty to say about the ceremony. “I actually enjoyed the pacing this year,” said Hilary Nonis, 30. “Sometimes it drags on but this time it felt more watchable.” Others, however, felt differently. “I still think it’s too long,” Amanda said.. “I watched the highlights the next day instead.” Despite mixed reactions, one thing is clear: the Oscars continue to hold a unique place in how we experience film. “It’s not just about who wins,” Tiwari said. “It’s about celebrating stories and performances that stay with us.” In an era of streaming platforms and endless content, the Academy Awards still manage to bring people together to celebrate cinema. And whether we agree with the winners or not, the conversations they spark remind us why we love films in the first place. Ultimately, the 98th Academy Awards were a mix of triumphs, snubs, debates and moments of inspiration. And for those of us who love cinema, this is exactly what keeps us tuning in. title: Surviving the horrendous Lankan heat
full_text: 21st March, 2026 18 Mar 2026 | BY Apsara Rodrigo 18 Mar 2026 | BY Apsara Rodrigo Sri Lanka is gearing up towards March and April. That means plenty of outdoor sports events, getting ready for religious and cultural festivals and of course surviving the scorching heat. “I often worry about facing the heat, especially since I have started to develop skin problems as a result,” Amaya De Silva, 27, told The Daily Morning Brunch. This follows the heat advisory warning issued by the Department of Meteorology for most of the provinces in Sri Lanka. Living in a furnace For many of us, the heat has become unbearable. We look forward to the time where we can finally stay under a fan or an air conditioned environment. Yet, this is not always a reality. “I have to travel to clean houses every day and I do not have the money to go by cab,” Lalitha Priyadarshini, 70, said. “And I often feel dizzy when I travel. I also have to walk for a good thirty minutes to get to the bus stop which makes me even worse.” Priyadarshini said that despite the discomfort, this is essential for her survival. “I have a husband who is unemployed,” Priyadarshini said. “He depends on the money so I have to work in the scorching heat.” Meanwhile, others struggle with similar physical and emotional challenges that come with the heat. “Despite the heat, my school thinks it’s a great idea to keep us out in the sun for assemblies,” Sonia Khan, 17, said. “I’ve seen a lot of kids faint during assembly because of this.” “Sometimes it feels like no one really understands how bad it gets,” Khan said. “We’re told to just stand there and not complain.” Her experience is not uncommon. Across the country, students and working professionals alike are finding themselves pushed to their limits as temperatures rise. A daily struggle For many office workers, the heat does not end once they step indoors. “I teach students and sometimes I find it difficult to live with this heat,” Kesha Kalpage, 32, said. “By midday, it feels stuffy and suffocating. It’s hard to concentrate on anything.” Kalpage said that productivity often takes a hit during the hottest hours of the day. “You feel tired, irritated and distracted. Even simple tasks start to feel overwhelming,” she said. For those who rely on public transport, the journey to and from work can be just as draining. “I take the bus every morning, and it’s packed,” Nirmala Jayasinghe, 24, said. “There’s barely any ventilation and you’re standing in the heat for over an hour. By the time I get to work, I already feel exhausted.” She added that the return journey is often worse. “In the evening, the buses are even more crowded. Everyone is tired and uncomfortable, and tempers are short.” University students are also struggling to cope. “Our lecture halls get extremely hot, especially in the afternoons,” Hasini Wickramarachchi, 19, said. “Sometimes the fans don’t work properly and it becomes difficult to even sit through a lecture.” She noted that the heat affects focus and participation. “You try to listen but your mind just drifts because you’re so uncomfortable,” she said. School students continue to face challenges as well. Finding small ways to cope Despite these challenges, many people have found small, practical ways to deal with the heat. “I always carry a large bottle of water with me,” Jayasinghe said. “It’s the only way I can get through the commute and the workday.” Others rely on simple adjustments to their routines. “I try to schedule my most important tasks in the morning,” Priyadarshini said. “By afternoon, it’s too hot to think clearly.” Students, too, are adapting in whatever ways they can. “I sit near the windows whenever possible,” Wickramarachchi said. “Even a little bit of airflow makes a difference.” At home, coping mechanisms continue. “I avoid studying during the afternoon,” she added. “It’s just not productive. I wait until the evening when it’s a bit cooler.” However, these small changes can only do so much. “No matter what we do, we still have to deal with it,” Priyadarshini said. “There’s no real escape from the heat.” A growing concern The rising temperatures are not just an inconvenience; they are becoming a serious public concern. “Drinking water frequently is important,” Dr. Deepal Perera, a pediatrician at the Lady Ridgeway Hospital said. “Dehydration is one of the biggest problems we are seeing right now.” He noted that both students and working adults are increasingly affected. “Excessive heat can lead to headaches, fainting as well as skin problems, especially among vulnerable groups like children and the elderly,” Perera said. Holding on through the heat As Sri Lanka moves deeper into its hottest months, resilience has become a quiet theme in everyday life. From crowded buses and overheated offices to stifling classrooms, the heat is something that cuts across age, profession, and circumstance. “I just try to get through the day,” Khan said. “That’s all you can really do.” The Morning is Sri Lanka’s foremost national news brand, delivering timely insights across politics, current affairs, sport, and entertainment. title: The Table of Seven
full_text: 21st March, 2026 17 Mar 2026 | By Dimithri Wijesinghe 17 Mar 2026 | By Dimithri Wijesinghe International Women’s Day is often marked by speeches, panel discussions, and celebratory posts across social media. But this year, Colombo witnessed a far more unusual tribute. At Luna Mews, a one-night dining experience titled ‘The Table of Seven’ brought together gastronomy and storytelling in a way that felt refreshingly original. Created in collaboration with Singaporean Chef Derrick Kwa, the event honoured seven Sri Lankan women whose cultural influence spans fields as varied as fashion, human rights, mountaineering, film, art, entertainment, and community building. Rather than recounting these women’s accomplishments in speeches or presentations, the evening attempted something far more creative: translating their impact into food. Each course in the seven-course menu was designed to reflect the essence of one of the women being celebrated. The concept itself felt both novel and intimate, an opportunity to experience stories not through words alone, but through ingredients, flavours, and thoughtful culinary interpretation. Chef Derrick, whose culinary career has taken him through Michelin-starred kitchens and restaurants featured on the World’s 50 Best Restaurants list, approached the project with careful research. Having previously served as Group Executive Chef for boutique luxury hotels in Sri Lanka, he spent time familiarising himself with the country’s ingredients and culinary traditions. For this particular menu, he immersed himself in the personal narratives of the women being honoured. “I try to make sure everything is grounded in strong research,” Chef Derrick explained. “I spent time diving into interviews these individuals have given to understand their beliefs and what they stand for in their own words. From there, I look for ingredients that can symbolise those ideas.” The result was a menu built not simply around taste, but around symbolism. Each ingredient on the plate had been chosen deliberately, connecting the dish to the woman who inspired it. The curated experience presented the guests with beautifully designed cards explaining the inspiration behind each dish. The cards detailed how particular ingredients and culinary elements were selected to reflect the achievements, personalities, or philosophies of the women being honoured. It set the tone for the night, one that invited diners to pause and consider the meaning behind each plate. In many ways, this simple gesture transformed the meal into something more reflective. Diners were not simply tasting a dish; they were engaging with a story. The setting itself played an important role in shaping the atmosphere. Known for hosting intimate cultural gatherings that merge art, conversation, and cuisine, Luna Mews provided a fitting backdrop for the event. The evening unfolded gradually, allowing guests to explore the space, take in the art around them, and settle into the rhythm of the meal. But perhaps the most special aspect of the evening was that the women who inspired the dishes were present at the table itself. Sharing the meal with the very individuals whose lives and work had informed the menu added a sense of immediacy to the experience. The storytelling moved beyond abstraction; it became personal and conversational. As the courses arrived, the menu moved through a sequence of distinct flavours and textures. Each dish felt intentionally composed, with ingredients layered carefully rather than dramatically. The courses differed noticeably from one another, maintaining a sense of progression across the evening. Among the mains, the duck dish stood out as one of the highlights of the meal. It carried a depth of flavour that felt balanced and considered without being overly elaborate. More broadly, the seven courses maintained a clear sense of identity, with each plate offering something slightly different from the last. Yet what made the meal memorable was less about any single dish and more about the thought behind the concept itself. Chef Derrick’s approach to cooking treats food as a medium for communication. Originally trained as a digital marketer before entering the culinary world, he views ingredients as a way of telling stories – a language that does not rely on words but on memory, symbolism, and shared experience. The idea of celebrating influential women through food could easily have become overly sentimental or performative. Instead, the evening maintained a thoughtful balance. The dishes remained grounded in flavour while quietly reflecting the themes and personalities they represented. In that sense, The Table of Seven felt less like a conventional tasting menu and more like an artistic collaboration between chef, space, and subject. Events like this are still relatively rare in Colombo’s dining scene. While themed dinners and pop-up menus have become increasingly common, this particular concept, celebrating cultural icons through ingredient-driven storytelling, felt both fresh and imaginative. As International Women’s Day celebrations continue to evolve, perhaps that is what made this experience stand out. It demonstrated that honouring influence and achievement does not always require a stage or a speech. Sometimes, a thoughtfully set table and a carefully composed meal can tell the story just as well. title: A taste of Punjab with Charcoal Tandoor Fire Grill
full_text: 21st March, 2026 17 Mar 2026 | By Apsara Rodrigo 17 Mar 2026 | By Apsara Rodrigo India’s flavors tell a story of their own – from the dosas of South India to the Dhal Makhni of the Punjab region, people across the world are obsessed with the flavours and diversity of Indian food. Colombo will soon see a new entrant into its Indian food scene with Charcoal Tandoor Fire Grill. The new restaurant, which already has a branch in Bangkok,is set to open in April with a host of Indian dishes. “We try to bring the best of Mughal cuisine to our kitchen,” Abhay Pardeshi, chef at the soon to open Indian restaurant, Charcoal Tandoor Fire Grill, told The Daily Morning Brunch. A love for Punjabi cuisine For Pardeshi, his love for cooking didn’t come from his job but from a generational love for food. “My father and my grandfather used to be in the culinary industry,” he said. “My grandfather used to be a chef in Saudi Arabia and my father used to feed the Indian army. I used to help in the kitchen and that is how I learned how to make dishes. My love for food comes from my family. It is something I was raised to do.” He then trained in the Indian city of Pune before gaining experience as a chef in countries like Indonesia and Singapore. “After that I travelled to Bangkok where I worked at Charcoal Tandor Grill and Mixology and gained nine years of experience there,” he said. Pardeshi said he now hopes to cater to the Sri Lankan market and those of us with an interest in Punjabi food. “We hope to bring what we have been doing in Bangkok to Sri Lanka and to the Sri Lankan customers,” he said. Serving in Sri Lanka Sri Lanka has long been a haven for tandoori cuisine. Whether it is our favorite biryanis or the well known tandoori chicken, tandoor food has long been a favorite. “We are like a modern Indian steakhouse," Pardeshi said. “We carry the old flavors but add a modern feel.” Pardesh presented Brunch with a platter of plantain and cassava chips featuring three authentic Indian chutneys including a tomato chutney and a mint chutney. The flavors were mild and a nod to Indian cuisine and reminded us of authentic Indian food we have had when traveling. We were also treated to a creative variation of a samosa with potato filling and ketchup, which would appeal to people who are fond of mild flavors. “We plan to use a lot of flavors when catering to our customers,” he said. Catering to customers Most of us have varying tastes and requirements when it comes to food. Whether it stems from religious beliefs, ethical considerations or personal preferences, all of us want to see the food we love catered to us. “While we are not halal as of now we do not serve pork and beef,” Pardeshi said. “We do have vegetarian dishes and some of our dishes are vegan as well.” Pardeshi highlighted the importance of catering to different consumers and highlighting the regional variations of India. “With every fifty kilometer distance, we see food preparation change,” he said,highlighting the diverse nature of Indian food. “I want to bring out the flavors that make it so delicious.” He also expressed interest in working with local ingredients, particularly Sri Lanka’s seafood and exploring how it could be incorporated into dishes prepared using tandoor techniques and flavors. For Pardeshi, cooking remains something deeply personal. He described it as a craft he feels strongly connected to, shaped by the time he spent learning from his family and years working in kitchens abroad. As Colombo’s dining scene continues to evolve, he said he hopes diners will be open to exploring the variety of Indian food and the stories behind the dishes he prepares. title: EMASL celebrates Int’l Women’s Day with High Tea at ITC Ratnadipa
full_text: 21st March, 2026 17 Mar 2026 17 Mar 2026 The Event Management Association of Sri Lanka (EMA) hosted a High Tea at Ahasa, ITC Ratnadipa, to mark International Women’s Day and recognise the contributions of women in the professional events industry. The afternoon brought together the Event Management Association members and industry stakeholders for networking, interaction and discussion. The gathering was attended by EMA President Saliya Weerasekera, Vice Presidents Bathiya Jayakody and Gayan Attanayake, and Secretary Uzmaan Ghouse. During the formal proceedings, Vice President Bathiya Jayakody delivered the keynote address, speaking on women’s empowerment and the evolving role of female leadership in the business sector. He also referred to the association’s commitment to supporting an inclusive professional environment where women can contribute as decision-makers and innovators in the industry. Secretary Uzmaan Ghouse delivered the closing remarks of the formal segment, addressing the gathering and acknowledging the participation of members and stakeholders present at the event. The programme concluded with a raffle draw, where a female participant received a food and beverage voucher from ITC Ratnadipa, the venue for the event. Participants attended in smart casual attire with pink and purple accents to mark the occasion and reflect the theme of International Women’s Day. title: Awareness of Leprosy: Coffee table book Beyond the Scars launched
full_text: 21st March, 2026 16 Mar 2026 | By Buddhika Samaraweera 16 Mar 2026 | By Buddhika Samaraweera A coffee table book titled Beyond the Scars , aimed at raising awareness about leprosy and addressing the stigma surrounding the disease in Sri Lanka, was launched recently at the Sapphire Hall of the Courtyard by Marriott Colombo City Centre. The publication, produced by the Alliance Development Trust (ADT), brings together personal stories, photography and lived experiences of individuals affected by leprosy. The launch event brought together development partners, health professionals, media representatives and advocates working to support communities affected by the disease. A notable moment during the event came when two members of self-help groups shared their personal experiences. Their testimonies offered a powerful reminder of the human realities behind the disease and the challenges individuals continue to face due to stigma and discrimination. One of the speakers, whose husband is affected by leprosy and who leads the Egodauyana self-help group in Moratuwa, spoke about her journey supporting her family and community with resilience and compassion. Another member, who is directly affected by leprosy and has experienced nerve damage and significant emotional distress, spoke openly about her struggles and her path towards recovery and acceptance. Their accounts left a strong impression on those present, helping to deepen understanding about the social challenges linked to the disease and the importance of community support alongside medical treatment. In recognition of their courage and lived experiences, the two self-help group members were honoured as the chief guests of the event. The first copies of the book were ceremonially presented to them by National Christian Evangelical Alliance of Sri Lanka (NCEASL) CEO Godfrey Yogaraja. The publication seeks to shed light on the realities faced by people affected by leprosy while encouraging greater empathy and awareness. Organisers said the initiative aims to encourage dialogue and promote early detection as Sri Lanka continues efforts to eliminate the disease. According to national estimates, nearly 1,300 new cases of leprosy are reported annually in the country. While treatment is available, many individuals continue to face social exclusion and discrimination within their communities. Speaking at the event, ADT representatives said addressing leprosy requires attention not only to its medical aspects but also to the social barriers that affect those living with the disease. “Leprosy is not only a health issue but also a social justice issue. Through this publication, we hope to amplify the voices of those who have endured stigma and discrimination, and remind society that dignity, acceptance and compassion must be central to our response,” they said. The book was made available for purchase at the launch for Rs. 6,000. The event also recognised the work of health sector partners, community workers and advocates who continue to support awareness programmes and improve access to treatment and care across Sri Lanka. Participants were invited to join ADT’s awareness initiatives as Goodwill Ambassadors, encouraging individuals to contribute to public education efforts and support communities affected by leprosy. The launch concluded with a networking session where attendees discussed opportunities to strengthen collaboration and support efforts aimed at building greater awareness and moving towards the long-term goal of a leprosy-free Sri Lanka. title: Fashion Forward
full_text: 21st March, 2026 16 Mar 2026 |  By Apsara Rodrigo 16 Mar 2026 |  By Apsara Rodrigo Fashion is an integral part of our expression. People across time and culture have often turned to fashion as a form of social or personal identity. This is why so many fashion enthusiasts and designers look forward to this year’s edition of Colombo Fashion Week presented by Mastercard. “We not only want to make a contribution to fashion but also to the arts and culture scene at large,” Colombo Fashion Week (CFW), Ajai Vir Singh, said at a press conference held on 11 March at Cinnamon Life. As this year’s CFW, which will take place on the 1 to the 4 April at Cinnamon Life City of Dreams, is expected to make a mark, organizers eye bringing Sri Lankan fashion and talent to a more global stage. Loving local fashion Sri Lanka, like any country, has had its own special fashion moments. From the traditional sarees and sarongs to shalwars, our ethnic wear has taken over both the traditional and modern fashion spaces. “One of our most popular styles is batik,” Singh said. “This year we are proud to announce our batik collective at CFW.” He said that the introduction of the Batik Collective is to encourage emerging Sri Lankan designers to develop a distinct identity and take the Sri Lankan culture forward. “It is time we take our unique identity to the world and create a good impression,” Singh said. Taking Sri Lanka to the world While South Asian fashion such as jhumkas and sandals have been adopted by western fashion houses, many ethnic styles are yet to be appreciated. This year’s edition is looked forward to by many as CFW continues its partnership with the BRICS International Fashion Federation, which would help us showcase our designs across the world. “Sri Lanka’s geostrategic advantage has always positioned it as a regional hub and our partnership with the BRICS International Federation reflects the importance of Sri Lanka being represented on international platforms,” he said. This year, the event will feature 30 designers, including international participants from South Africa, India, and China. Singh noted that this mix will create opportunities for networking, collaboration, and cross-cultural exchange. Emerging designers are a key focus of CFW. Many are experimenting with sustainable fabrics, ethical production and modern interpretations of Sri Lankan designs. “The next generation of designers is thinking about aesthetics, sustainability, cultural relevance, and global appeal simultaneously,” Singh said. The event gives these designers visibility, potential buyers, and opportunities for international collaboration. “We want to empower designers to create with confidence, knowing their work will be appreciated locally and internationally,” Singh said. CFW shows that Sri Lankan design is versatile and competitive internationally. The inclusion of designers from South Asia and other regions adds new ideas and cross-cultural inspiration while providing the opportunity for our designers to interact and learn about global fashion and culture. “Fashion is a universal language,” Singh said. “When designers from different countries come together, we create dialogue through creativity.” With global designers, global partnerships and a venue that supports both creativity and hospitality, this year’s edition is expected to make a strong impact. “Our hope is that attendees leave inspired, designers leave motivated and that ri Lankan fashion leaves an imprint on the global stage,” Singh said. CFW continues to bridge tradition and innovation, creating a space where culture, creativity, and collaboration converge. title: Above the clouds: A Sri Lankan’s ascent of Mount Kilimanjaro
full_text: 21st March, 2026 16 Mar 2026 | By Nirmal Wijesinghe 16 Mar 2026 | By Nirmal Wijesinghe There are journeys that begin as adventure and end as transformation. This one began in Colombo, at sea level, in the comfort of warm tropical air. It ended at 5,895 metres above sea level on the summit of Mount Kilimanjaro where I stood at sunrise holding the Sri Lankan flag above a sea of clouds. Between those two points lay seven days of thin air, freezing temperatures, broken sleep, and one of the hardest physical and mental challenges of my life. This expedition was organised and led by Summit Nomads founder Nirmal Wijesinghe, with one clear purpose: to take Sri Lankans beyond comfort and into the world’s most demanding landscapes. As a Sri Lankan entrepreneur committed to high-altitude exploration, I have always believed that our limits exist to be challenged. Kilimanjaro was not just a climb it was a statement of what Sri Lankans can achieve on the world stage. Day 1: Shira Camp 1 – 3,500 Metres We began from Shira Camp 1 (3,500 m). At that altitude alone, the air already holds significantly less oxygen than at sea level. For many people, 3,500 metres is higher than they will ever stand in their lifetime. From the first afternoon, breathing required attention. Movements became slower. The mountain had already begun its quiet test. Day 2: Cathedral Point – 3,872 metres | Shira Camp 2 – 3,850 metres On the second day, we climbed to Cathedral Point at approximately 3,872 metres, one of the highest points on the Shira Plateau. The views across Tanzania were vast and humbling. We then continued to Shira Camp 2 (3,850 m) for the night. Though the numbers may seem close, every meter gained was felt in our lungs. Breathing was no longer automatic. It was deliberate. Day 3: Lava Tower – 4,600 metres | Barranco Camp – 3,960 metres Day 3 was a turning point. We climbed steadily to Lava Tower (4,600 m), a massive volcanic rock formation rising from the mountain’s slopes. At 4,600 metres, the altitude makes its presence known. Headaches began. My legs felt heavy. Conversations became shorter. After reaching the tower, we descended to Barranco Camp (3,960 m) to sleep lower and allow our bodies to adjust.The mountain follows a strict rule: climb high, sleep low. That night, the cold intensified. We slept in tents, but the sleep was shallow and broken. Ice formed inside the tent from our own breath. In the morning, frozen crystals lined the inner walls. There was no real rest, only endurance. Day 4: Barranco Wall – 4,200 metres | Karanga Camp – 4,035 metres On Day 4, we faced the imposing Barranco Wall, rising to about 4,200 metres at its highest point. From below, it appears almost vertical. Climbers move slowly, gripping rock with hands and boots, focused on every step. One mistake could mean serious injury. Reaching the top of the wall felt like a victory of its own. But Kilimanjaro does not offer easy wins. We continued to Karanga Camp (4,035 m) for the night. The air was thinner. The wind was colder. The summit now felt close but still far. Day 5: Barafu Camp – 4,673 metres On Day 5, we moved to Barafu Camp (4,673 m) – the final base before the summit attempt. Barafu means “ice” in Swahili. The name is fitting. The camp is exposed, rocky, and unforgiving. No one truly sleeps at that altitude. You lie down knowing that midnight will come quickly. Six nights without proper sleep had taken a toll. The cold had settled deep into the body. Day 6: Summit Night - Uhuru Peak – 5,895 metres At exactly 12 midnight, we began the final ascent toward Uhuru Peak (5,895 m). The temperature dropped sharply. The wind cut through every layer of clothing. As we climbed higher, I reached for water but it had already frozen inside my hydration pack. Even drinking was no longer possible. At nearly 6,000 metres, oxygen levels are roughly half of what they are at sea level. Every ten steps required a pause. Breathing was slow and controlled. Around me, climbers struggled. I saw people collapse from low oxygen. I saw others sit down, unable to continue. Porters carried exhausted trekkers on their backs because they could not descend on their own. At that altitude, strength alone is not enough. Your body fights. Your mind negotiates. After hours of slow movement in darkness, the sky began to lighten. Black turned to deep blue. Then the first gold light appeared on the horizon. At sunrise, I stood on the highest point in Africa, atop the world’s tallest free-standing peak, 5,895 metres above sea level. Above the clouds. Above exhaustion. Above doubt. I held the Sri Lankan flag high against the rising African sun. In that moment, the cold, the frozen water, the sleepless nights, and the pain faded into silence. Descent: Mweka Camp – 3,100 metres | Mweka Gate – 1,640 metres The summit is only halfway. We descended back to Barafu Camp and continued down to Mweka Camp (3,100 m). With every meter lost, oxygen slowly returned. Muscles burned. Knees trembled. On Day 7, we reached Mweka Gate (1,640 m). The climb was complete. What the Mountain teaches From 3,500 metres at Shira Camp 1 to 5,895 metres at Uhuru Peak, Kilimanjaro is not just a mountain, it is a test of endurance, patience, and mental strength. You live in a tent. You endure sub-zero temperatures. You function on little sleep. You witness how fragile the human body becomes at altitude. But you also discover something greater.The human spirit is stronger than the mountain. When I left Colombo, this was an expedition. When I returned, it was a reminder: the greatest summits we conquer are often the ones within ourselves. And sometimes, they stand 5,895 metres above the clouds. (The writer is a high-altitude expedition leader and adventurer, and is the Founder...

title: ‘Winds Are Not Just Shifting; They Are Strengthening’
full_text: 21st March, 2026 20 Mar 2026 | BY Vindya Amaranayake 20 Mar 2026 | BY Vindya Amaranayake As the ongoing conflict in the Middle East sends ripples across the Indian Ocean, most notably with the attack on the Iranian warship IRIS Dena , fresh geopolitical concerns are emerging for the region. Against this backdrop of rising tensions and shifting global dynamics, The Daily Morning spoke with geopolitical analyst Asanga Abeyagoonasekera on the wider implications for South Asia and Sri Lanka. Abeyagoonasekera is a Sri Lankan geopolitics and international security analyst, academic, and author known for his work on foreign policy, regional security, and global power dynamics. He has written extensively on Sri Lanka’s strategic position in an evolving global order, with notable works including Sri Lanka at Crossroads (2019), Conundrum of an Island (2021), and Teardrop Diplomacy (2023). His latest book, Winds of Change: Geopolitics at the Crossroads of South and Southeast Asia , examines a region in transition amid intensifying global competition. In this interview, he reflects on shifting global power structures, the growing strategic importance of the Indian Ocean, and how external conflicts are increasingly shaping regional stability, while also assessing Sri Lanka’s place at the centre of an increasingly contested geopolitical landscape. Following are excerpts of the interview Your latest work, Winds of Change: Geopolitics at the Crossroads of South and Southeast Asia examines a region in transition. In light of the current conflicts in the Middle East and rising tensions in the Indian Ocean, do you believe those “winds of change” are accelerating faster than you anticipated? Yes — and significantly so. The pace of geopolitical disruption today is moving faster than many recent projections. Conflicts in the Middle East, rising maritime insecurity in the Indian Ocean, and intensifying US–China competition are compressing strategic timelines. What once appeared as gradual power transition is now rapid recalibration. There are multiple theatres, Ukraine, Latin America, Taiwan and Middle East. For Sri Lanka and the wider region, this means that decisions which used to have long-term implications now have immediate strategic consequences. The winds are not merely shifting direction — they are strengthening. We are all, in many ways, living under the influence of geopolitics. The world may appear vast, but it is shaped by narrow maritime corridors and critical choke points — such as the Strait of Hormuz — that directly affect energy flows, food prices, supply chains, and ultimately daily life. What happens in distant waters can influence what reaches our kitchens, our workplaces, and our economies. Winds of Change is part of a broader trilogy, following Sri Lanka at Crossroads and Conundrum of an Island . Across these works, I have attempted to map the geo-strategic headwinds facing a small island state and its surrounding region amid major power rivalry. Whether we engage with geopolitics or not, geopolitics engages with us. With conflicts in the Middle East, tensions in Europe, and growing strategic competition in the Indo-Pacific, are we witnessing a fundamental shift in the global order? We are in the midst of a transition — from a system that was largely unipolar-leaning toward a more fragmented multipolar order. The combined pressures of war in Europe, instability in the Middle East, and strategic competition in the Indo-Pacific are reshaping alliances, trade corridors, energy security, and maritime strategy. Rather than a single dominant global architecture, we are seeing overlapping spheres of influence and competing regional frameworks. This shift is structural, not temporary. It reflects deeper geopolitical realignments that extend beyond individual conflicts. At the same time, it is important to recognise that the US operates with long-standing institutional structures. Presidential transitions influence tone and priorities, but core strategic machinery often remains consistent. In my book, I also engage with the debate shaped by my teacher, Professor Kishore Mahbubani, whose work emphasises the structural rise of China. The data and field observations I documented across Southeast Asia and South Asia — from Cambodia, Vietnam, and Myanmar to India and its neighbourhood — indicate a noticeable shift in regional perceptions. There is growing strategic recalibration, and in some cases, a trust deficit toward traditional Western leadership, alongside increasing engagement with Asian civilisational powers. This does not suggest an absolute decline of one power or the inevitable rise of another. You outline how external conflicts increasingly shape regional stability. With the ongoing Middle East conflict now affecting energy flows and maritime routes, how do you see its spillover impacting South Asia and, specifically, Sri Lanka? The Middle East conflict has significant spillover effects on South Asia and Sri Lanka. Rising oil prices directly impact Sri Lanka’s import-dependent economy, increasing transport costs, inflation, and pressure on households. As energy costs rise, the impact spreads across the broader economy. Maritime insecurity in the Indian Ocean further increases shipping costs and insurance premiums, reducing trade competitiveness and raising the cost of both imports and exports. For a trade-dependent island nation, such disruptions have immediate consequences. At the same time, Sri Lanka must navigate complex diplomatic realities. For Sri Lanka — as an island economy positioned at the heart of the Indian Ocean — maritime stability is not optional A key theme in your work is the growing importance of the Indian Ocean. Given the recent incidents, including attacks on vessels near Sri Lankan territorial waters, are we witnessing the Indian Ocean increasingly becoming an active geopolitical theatre? Yes. The Indian Ocean is no longer simply a passive trade corridor — it is increasingly becoming an active geopolitical theatre. For decades, it functioned primarily as a commercial maritime highway. Today, however, it is also a space of strategic competition, naval presence, surveillance activity, and power projection. We are witnessing increased naval deployments and heightened attention around critical chokepoints that carry a significant share of global energy flows and trade. The security of sea lanes and undersea communication cables has become central to geopolitical calculations. Any instability in these waters has consequences far beyond the region. Sri Lanka’s geographic position places it directly within this...

title: Empowering communities, one child at a time
full_text: 21st March, 2026 23 Feb 2026 | BY Savithri Rodrigo 23 Feb 2026 | BY Savithri Rodrigo In 2024, the World Vision Lanka organisation reached nearly 100,000 children and families across the island, delivering vital support in health, nutrition, education, livelihoods, and climate resilience. But, behind every figure is a story: a child who gets a proper meal, a family earning a sustainable income, a community learning to weather disasters. On Kaleidoscope this week was the World Vision Lanka National Director Dr. Dhanan Senathirajah, who unpacked these numbers, revealing the real impact, exploring how data translates into lasting change. Following are excerpts of the interview: You have 8,491 new sponsors. How does World Vision’s new sponsors translate into real impact for the nearly 100,000 children? The funds the sponsors send don’t go directly to the children or to the child’s family. They go for the benefit of the community itself, in the sense that if there are, for example, 6,000 children in a programme in a single area, the funding goes to benefit all the children in that single programme. If we build a school, it not only benefits the sponsored children; it also benefits all the children in that community. In effect, on average, a sponsored child’s contribution will help at least another eight children. Over 6,500 people were helped in the health and nutrition area. What were the biggest improvements that you saw, and what were the gaps that you observed? Primarily, working along with the Government, we identified the malnutrition-related aspect early, so we try to bring about a mindset change in the parents themselves, in particular the mothers, to address the issue of malnutrition by going for sustainable methodologies. For instance, we look for food or food sources that are locally available, which tend to be cheaper and more environmentally friendly. And then, we have a follow-up programme with the parents, along with the Government authorities of that area. What does the improvement of water, sanitation and hygiene services, for instance, mean for community resilience? Water and sanitation are not about convenience only; they’re about self-respect as well. As we know, water is life, it’s life-giving. At the same time, it’s also about the dignity of the community. If you have access to proper drinking water, as well as water for cleaning and washing, then it adds to their dignity. It allows children more time to sit and study and spend more time on their education. In the past, we have had many water, sanitation and hygiene projects. One of our largest projects was in two phases in the South, in Neluwa, which was funded by a UK bank. It was for an amount of US $ 1.35 million. And this was, or still is, the largest gravity-flow water project in Sri Lanka’s history. It provides water for at least 2,000 families, plus Government offices, Police stations, and hospitals, as well as schools and temples. World Vision Lanka also initiates a number of economic initiatives – savings groups, income programmes, and producer groups. How has that translated into a sustainable income for families? These tools are primarily to help communities become independent. My favourite is savings groups. Generally, more than 95 per cent of the savings group members are females. Many of them have never worked before or had any source of income. They are totally dependent on their husbands and sometimes that can also lead to domestic abuse within the home. These women are now becoming independent, and they’re able to save. About 90% of them invest in sustainable business models – small businesses. As a result, they become independent, their dignity is improved, and in addition to that, there are enough resources for their children’s education. Right now, we have 2,000 active savings groups and 40,000 women actively participating in them. Over a period, the total savings have amounted to Rs. 4 billion. They also have a social fund to help others, which now stands at Rs. 42 million. Is World Vision doing anything to tackle food insecurity? Where are you seeing food insecurity, especially in which Districts? In Sri Lanka, food insecurity is not at the same level across the country. It is primarily in the North, to a certain extent in the East, and very high in the plantation sector. So that’s where we have concentrated a lot of effort on improving food security. We have gone in for climate-smart agriculture, and in addition to that, home gardening as well. These initiatives really help families become sustainable in their food intake. In fact, a recent survey shows that 51% of households have either reduced their food intake or cut down on the number of meals since the 2022 crisis. This is where we have been able to make an impact. You have 35 impact-class youth clubs, training adolescents as well as community peace builders. How are you measuring that long-term social impact? We have seen an enormous impact in these areas. Young people are now able to come together and relate to one another, build relationships and solve conflicts without violence. We have also kept many of them away from dangerous substances like alcohol, tobacco, and drugs. They are able to build confidence, life skills, communication skills, and soft skills. All of these have worked out in these communities. The main advantage is these youngsters are now beginning to advocate for their rights and for the rights of the community. These children are able to advocate and express their needs and the kind of world they want to live in. How effective have the climate smart programmes been in reducing disaster risks? Disaster risk cannot be reduced or eliminated solely by climate-smart programmes. What they do is help communities recover quickly after a disaster situation. Even after Cyclone Ditwah and earlier climate-related emergencies, communities have bounced back because they are using the resources that are available. A lot of their focus is on soil preservation and water sources. And that has helped...

title: Grand Final; Straight up Rugby analysis
full_text: 21st March, 2026 20 Feb 2026 | BY Muaard Razick 20 Feb 2026 | BY Muaard Razick Folks we are just one sleep away from the most anticipated game of this year’s Inter-Club Rugby League - the Grand Final Playoff as arch-rivals Ceylonese Rugby and Football Club (CR and FC) play host to defending Kandy Sports Club on Saturday (21) 7.00 p.m. at Longden Place, the home of the ‘red shirts’. CR having a 2-0 win advantage will meet the hill capital team yearning for glory. This high stakes final means renewing yet another chapter in the perennial rivalry in what’s considered a classic Colombo-Kandy clash between two of the strongest teams of the island’s club rugby scene. Tomorrow’s encounter becomes the third time these two outfits go head to head against each other in a single League season making it arguably the first such occasion. One for the history books indeed. It is advisable to go to the grounds as early as possible to grab your seats, and it is expected to be a chockablock affair when it comes to catch a parking space as all roads lead to Longden Place on Saturday night. In the midst of all the chaos and excitement surrounding the biggest game in Sri Lanka’s club rugby calendar, we spoke to Boa Athu, World Rugby coach educator with New Zealand Rugby (NZR) and founder of ‘Straight up Rugby’ all the way from Auckland about the ins and outs of the upcoming Grand Final playoff. Following are excerpts of the interview How important is this grand final playoff for rugby in general in Sri Lanka? Of course it is the grand finale, it's the big dance. It's important that everyone puts on a great entertaining show both on and off the field. From an administrator's perspective you've got a new Executive which has come in and it's a great opportunity to make a grand impression for the fans and from a fan engagement point of view it is going to be very important. And of course if it is backed up by having an absolute nail-biting grand final you know it's going to create great memories and that's what I think is the most important thing. It's got to be memorable, so this is everyone's chance to really make what you call a grand impression on a grand final day. Hopefully everything goes to plan and we see some great footy on the field and it's a close game which will be talked about for quite a while to come. What are your thoughts on the present day Kandy-CR rivalry compared to the golden era? Yeah, I think it needs a bit of a kickstart and maybe it’s already happening because at the moment the power at least for this season seems to have shifted to the Longden Place club and that’s a good thing, because we can’t have one club winning all the time, there needs to be competitiveness. So I think the CR-Kandy rivalry is pretty much enshrined, hopefully this weekend’s final will give it another boost and send it to a whole new different level. Yes CR earned that home final beating Kandy 2-0. But do you think a Nittawela final would've been an absolute banger? Nittawela is always the place to be and place to watch rugby, and of course with this rivalry, playing at the fortress would have been amazing, but I think CR have really earned the right to host this final and no one should ever deny them that, they’ve earned it. So, the reality is, Nittawela and the fortress, as far as this game goes, is irrelevant. CR has the home edge, hopefully all the Kandy fans turn up and the next thing you know, it turns into a real spectacle. How did Kandy lose the plot on both occasions in the opening and super round against CR? Kandy Sports Club has been a bit inconsistent, and up and down they’ve really haven’t put out a complete performance to date this season. It was just errors and lots of mistakes. That’s what has been holding them back and that’s what’s costing them. And of course not to forget, the goal-kicking, that has let them down pretty badly. Your views on CR's back to back success against Kandy this season even after losing a game to CH and Havies in the process? CR looks like they're a side who do the basics well. They've got good structure to the game and they try to physically dominate the opposition. So I think that’s been the key and of course the goal kicking. I’ve been going on and on about this for many, many seasons and many years, and it looks like they’ve been listening and they got it right at least this time around. Key Takeaways for CR and FC's Success What do you think is the mantra for winning crucial grand finals? Strategies for Winning a Rugby Finale Overall season 2025/26, do you believe this is one of the most interesting League seasons after a long hiatus? Overall I think it's been a good enough season, but again there's a lot to improve on as far as the quality of rugby and the ability to play continuous, or what we call continuity in rugby. So that needs to be addressed and once that is addressed, we’re going to see more entertaining rugby and you'll see more crowds come as well. But it's important that we build from this and this is almost like a restarter, so let's see how things pan out. What are the key areas to develop for both finalists? The easiest way to win a final, on top of what I have given you (checklist), is to make sure you keep your discipline, give the least amount of penalties, no cards. And then you just don't put pressure on yourself. What should be the next key plan for Sri Lanka Rugby looking into the future...

title: ‘At Hyde Park’ with Indeewari Amuwatte on Ada Derana: ‘We are not your grandmother's IMF anymore’ – Dr. Kristalina Gorgieva
full_text: 21st March, 2026 18 Feb 2026 18 Feb 2026 Managing Director of the International Monetary Fund (IMF), Dr. Kristalina Gorgieva, is in Sri Lanka on her first official visit to the country at a critical juncture of the Sri Lankan economy's path to recovery after severe blows on the economy, be it financial, external, terrorism-related or even climate-related, after the most recent shock from Cyclone Ditwah . As Sri Lanka continues the IMF-supported reform programme, global uncertainty posing risks on not just Sri Lanka but other smaller economies, Indeewari Amuwatte speaks with Dr. Gorgieva ‘At Hyde Park’ aired on Ada Derana , about progress made, risks ahead and what this moment means for the country's future. Following are excerpts of the interview: Dr. Gorgieva, this is your first visit to Sri Lanka. What does it represent to you and what does it means symbolically and more practically for us in Sri Lanka, in terms of the journey ahead in Sri Lanka’s recovery progress, and what would you highlight as the most important and significant gains the country has made since the programme began with the IMF? I am really very pleased to come because the country has made such tremendous progress since the days when the country was close to collapse and congratulations to the Government and the people of Sri Lanka for the progress made. But I am also glad that I can be here soon after the cyclone that devastated parts of the country as an expression of solidarity and a chance for me to witness the strength of the Sri Lankan people, the resilience you demonstrate and the unity you so clearly achieve in this moment of tremendous challenge for the country. You visited the Ditwah affected areas and the residents. Does what Sri Lanka has gone through, mean something personal to you? I had a chance to meet with the community affected by Ditwah and see that people remain strong, after the horror of what has happened to them. I used to be a humanitarian commissioner and I visited many places devastated by natural disasters and conflicts and what I can say is that you have wonderful people. They come together at a moment of tremendous pain and they think of the future. I also am very impressed by how the Government stepped forward. Quickly and decisively. We at the IMF were able to provide emergency financing, of over US $ 200 million which has come in very timely. So these communities are not to bearing the pain of what has happened to them alone. And I’ll tell you, looking at the faces of the children, it fills me with hope for the country, because I can see the character of Sri Lanka, is still warm and generous, with your welcome, even when the community has gone through so much. Sri Lanka has had severe blows to the economy in the last 10 years after the end of the war, whether it is global uncertainty, external, political or climate related shocks. You met the President of Sri Lanka and during this very brief visit you made time to meet cyclone affected residents. What has been your message to the political leaders and authorities as Sri Lanka remains resilient to these shocks? Indeed, Sri Lanka has had quite a challenge in the last decade. It has gone through civil war, there was a terrorist attack, the pandemic hit you and in 2004 the tsunami hit the country and after that there was social unrest because of the sense the Government was falling short in meeting the aspirations of the people. Now we have a tremendous opportunity for the country. I think for the first time there is a Government that is trusted by the people and is doing their part to gain that trust. I want to say from our experience, one, in a short time Sri Lanka regained macroeconomic and financial stability. Inflation which was 70%, now is down to 2%. Growth was negative, now we have about 5% growth. And very important, the view of the Government is that there has to be trust in civil service, and that means corruption has to be eliminated. Sri Lanka is the first country in Asia in which the Government asked us to do a governance assessment so we can help in this fight to eliminate corruption. I also saw in the President and in the members of the Government genuine interest in the well-being of the people. And I know that success comes when the country is united. This Government is working so hard to achieve that. That also takes us back to when Sri Lanka decided to agree to an IMF-supported programme. Sri Lanka's decision was controversial and was met with public and political resistance. From where we are speaking today, in moments acute crisis, countries like ours, frontier markets like Sri Lanka, face limited choices. From your experience, how do you view that decision from the significant recovery Sri Lanka has made, as you describe today? Well, the IMF has changed over the decades. We are not your grandmother's IMF anymore. We pay a lot of attention to people in our programmes. We actually request Governments to set the floor for social spending. So education, health care, social protection, don't suffer when a country goes through a problem. We currently have 50 programmes. Why so many? Because we live in a world that is changing very rapidly. It is a more shock-prone world. And more countries require the financial support from the IMF. Think of it as your right. Your right in a moment of difficulty. Not to be left on your own. To have a helping hand and to have the expertise of IMF staff to help the country turn its course for the better. We have 191 members. We learn from each and every one of them. And we pass this experience. We are like a transmission line of best practice from one...

title: SL needs to foster self-betterment
full_text: 21st March, 2026 16 Feb 2026 | BY Savithri Rodrigo 16 Feb 2026 | BY Savithri Rodrigo In 2025, Sri Lanka’s foreign direct investment (FDI) was at US $ 1.3 billion, with exports still dominated by tea and apparel. While the recently announced International Finance Corporation (IFC) financing package of $ 166 million to the private sector does showcase confidence in Sri Lanka, what will it take to attract sustainable investment and stability? Bringing expertise in private, official and blended financing for climate and development, on Kaleidoscope was the IFC Manager for Country Advisory and Economics South Asia, Gregory Smith, discussing what it will take to unlock potential for Sri Lanka. Following are excerpts of the interview: The IFC announced a $ 66 million financing package for the private sector, primarily for female-led businesses and agribusinesses. Does this mean that Sri Lanka is being viewed favourably by funders like the IFC? Absolutely. We’re very excited about that investment, and it’s one of many that we’ve been working on. It’s certainly linked to the strength of the economy over the last few years. You’ve said that Sri Lanka needs at least a decade of stability to unlock sustainable growth. Given Sri Lanka’s history of political and policy reversals, what specifically must change for this stability to be credible rather than aspirational? That macro stability of at least 10 years would be the backbone of an economy that grows much faster towards the aspirational rate of seven per cent per annum, from around 4% or 5% a year. First, there needs to be a political commitment to maintaining macro stability. Legislation helps. Strong laws that prevent macro excesses in certain years — and a strong, independent Central Bank that will provide the stability that the economy needs. But, added to that is a demand from voters who want to vote for macro stability so their businesses can thrive, and they can maintain their welfare. Investors remember shocks. So, what signals would convince them that this recovery will stick? You earn trust back over the months and years. And, what we’ve had so far is good reform and growth, certainly in 2024 and certainly in 2025. As these quarterly prints keep coming in, showing that the economy is positive, that will give the investors’ confidence. Also, the fact that we’re going to see structural reforms to cement some of the decent reforms. And, at the moment, at least the presence of the International Monetary Fund (IMF), or international investors, is a real positive. In a democracy that’s under fiscal strain, how realistic is long-term discipline? It’s not easy, but, it’s certainly doable. Lots of countries have achieved that. East Asian countries went through a lot of crises in the late ’90s, and they decided, not again. We’re not going to be dependent on external debt in the same way. We’re going to build up our buffers in the form of foreign exchange reserves, and we’re going to implement policies. Another example in South Asia (SA) is India, which in the ’90s decided that it wasn’t going to have another macro crisis and went about a serious set of reforms that’s led to macro stability since then. Beyond tea and apparel, where should Sri Lanka be placing its next big bets? There’s massive room for growth in agribusiness, going beyond the traditional crops to high-value goods that can be consumed both here in Sri Lanka and exported. There are so many flights these days to the Gulf and other geographies that could be packed with Sri Lankan goods for export. So, rather than going to supermarkets in Colombo and buying foreign agricultural produce, Sri Lankan produce can instead be in supermarkets in the Gulf and other places. That would be a real way of not only helping exports, strengthening the economy, and also lead to a lot of job creation. Would Sri Lanka be better off focusing on a few niche industries, a few niche strengths, rather than trying to do everything and competing in the bigger sphere of things? What first needs to happen is there needs to be an effort to secure a more competitive business environment. If it were more competitive, on that backbone of macro stability, it could be left to entrepreneurs and business people to choose where they know they can make money and where they can make things more productive. Do you think that we really need to compete with India and Vietnam, or similar countries? Or, should we just play a different game altogether? These are different countries. They’re much larger countries, particularly India. So, Sri Lanka needs to do what’s best for Sri Lanka. There are things that can be borrowed from other countries’ ideas, but, Sri Lanka certainly needs to be in value chains and competing with some products in both those markets. Are global supply chain shifts a risk for Sri Lanka, or do you see them as an opportunity? They’re both. They create a lot of uncertainty, which makes it difficult for investors in particular sectors. But, at the same time, with big shifts in trade patterns, there are certain opportunities that Sri Lanka is well positioned to exploit. What are the biggest mistakes that post-crisis countries make? For countries that have defaulted on their sovereign debt, this can happen once and then again and again. There’s this terrible term called ‘serial defaulter’ for countries that haven’t managed to get their finances in shape. This can lead to depressed growth and a depressed economy for many years, even decades. Some countries have turned a page and reformed, and some countries have gone back into that trap of debt. And, one hopes the ongoing reforms in Sri Lanka will set the country on a path to better prosperity. You have expertise in blended finance. Why has blended finance been harder to deploy here than expected? Blended finance is great, but, it’s also in short supply. There isn’t a lot of it, so, countries are...

title: Protecting SL’s archaeological heritage: Genuine, non-political commitment needed
full_text: 21st March, 2026 16 Mar 2026 | BY Dilanthi Jayamanne 16 Mar 2026 | BY Dilanthi Jayamanne The vision of the All Ceylon Buddhist Congress (ACBC) is to promote the teachings of the Buddha and Buddhist philosophy. In 2026, it convened a gathering of the Sangha, calling on the Government to focus on 10 key factors to guide it towards righteous governance. It emphasised that recognising Buddhism and the Sangha’s lineage as the foundation of Sri Lanka is not extremism but part of the country’s identity. The Government, it said, must acknowledge the Maha Sangha offers guidance not for votes, but for the long-term stability of the country and the Sasana. The ACBC also advocated respect for majority sentiments, when the beliefs and heritage of the 70 per cent majority are dismissed as outdated, it leads to alienation from governance. Protecting the cultural rights of the majority is a key responsibility of the State. The political use of religious leaders must cease, proposals presented at Sangha assemblies should be understood and implemented. Understanding rather than criticising that Sangha assemblies are not convened to oppose the Government but to guide it. While criticism escalates problems, dialogue and acceptance lead to solutions. It also highlighted the need for social justice and morality. The Government alone cannot curb the country’s moral decline caused by corruption, drugs, and crime, and the guidance of the Maha Sangha is essential in this regard. The Charter of the Great Sangha Conference held this year called on the Government to accept this reality and refrain from criticism, stressing that rulers must set aside personal ego and respect the country’s true heritage. Such governance, it said, would earn the respect of both the majority and the minorities. Amid growing concern over challenges faced by the clergy, The Daily Morning spoke with the ACBC’s Working Committee’s Vice President (VP) and the Chairperson of the ACBC’s National Council for the Investigation of National and Buddhist Affairs, Ishan Buddhika Edirisuriya on key issues affecting the religion. Following are excerpts of the interview In recent years, there has not been any similar conference of the Sangha, so what were the reasons for holding one now? The last conference was held in 2019. We then issued a charter titled the ‘Maha Sangha Conference’. Ten key proposals were presented at the Congress to create awareness within the Government, and a similar Sangha charter was issued at this year’s gathering as well. In 1955, the ACBC was formally established after it was legally recognised through an Act of Parliament. With this recognition, it received the authority to act on matters concerning Buddhism. Its main objective was to protect the Buddha Sasana, support the Maha Sangha, defend the rights of the Buddhist people, and speak out against injustice or actions that may harm them. This demonstrated the clear distinction between political power and the independent role of the Congress. The present role of the Congress remains the same. We work with any Government while representing the aspirations of the Buddhist majority. In particular, we are committed to safeguarding the protections given to Buddhism under the 1972 and 1978 Constitutions, especially Article 9.
