title: Seylan opens its 290th ‘Pahasara’ library
body: Saturday Mar 21, 2026 Dailymirror Sunday Times Mirror Edu Tamil Mirror Lankadeepa Middleast Lankadeepa Ada Deshaya Life Online Hi Online Hitad Timesjobs E-Paper Home delivery Advertise with us Print Ads WNL Home Friday, 13 March 2026 00:04 - - {{hitsCtrl.values.hits}} Seylan Bank Director/CEO Ramesh Jayasekara at the opening of the "Pahasara library" at Sujatha Balika Vidyalaya Seylan Bank PLC marked another milestone in its flagship Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) initiative by opening its 290th ‘Pahasara’ library at Sujatha Balika Vidyalaya. The ceremony was held on 11 February 2026 with Seylan Bank Director/Chief Executive Officer Ramesh Jayasekara, Assistant Director of Education Chintha Perera (Zonal Education Office – Colombo) and Sujatha Balika Vidyalaya’s Deputy Principal O K D M Lakshanthi in attendance. The newly refurbished library was officially handed over following the completion of ‘Pahasara Week’, a series of student-focused engagement activities conducted jointly by Seylan Bank and the school. As part of the initiative, the bank donated books along with a laptop and smart board to support learning and digital access. The ‘Pahasara Week’ included a Math Camp for both primary and upper school students, a Cooking Day judged by a panel of reputed chefs, and a financial literacy program aimed at introducing children to savings habits and basic money management from an early age. Students also actively participated in maintaining the library, helping build responsibility and management skills during school life. Speaking on the occasion, Seylan Bank AGM Marketing and Sales Asiri Abhayaratne said: “The opening of the 290th library represents a significant step as the bank moves steadily toward its commitment of establishing 300 ‘Pahasara’ libraries across the country. Seylan Bank remains committed to funding library refurbishments and providing essential educational resources to cultivate students’ knowledge and skills.” Education officials, teachers, parents, and students extended their full cooperation to ensure the success of the program, with strong student participation recorded throughout ‘Pahasara Week’ activities. The Seylan ‘Pahasara’ Library Project, launched on 1 March 2013, stands as Seylan Bank’s long-term social sustainability initiative, placing education at the centre of empowering future generations. Through this program, Seylan Bank continues to support schools islandwide by strengthening library infrastructure and expanding access to essential learning tools. As the Bank moves steadily towards its landmark 300th ‘Pahasara’ Library, this milestone represents one of the biggest achievements in the ‘Pahasara’ journey, reinforcing Seylan Bank’s commitment to building knowledge, nurturing financial literacy, and creating lasting community impact across Sri Lanka. title: Cambridge drives new era of digital maturity for age of AI
body: Saturday Mar 21, 2026 Dailymirror Sunday Times Mirror Edu Tamil Mirror Lankadeepa Middleast Lankadeepa Ada Deshaya Life Online Hi Online Hitad Timesjobs E-Paper Home delivery Advertise with us Print Ads WNL Home Friday, 13 March 2026 00:02 - - {{hitsCtrl.values.hits}} As digital literacy moves to the forefront of education reforms worldwide, the International Education group at Cambridge University Press & Assessment has announced a major transformation of its Cambridge Primary and Lower Secondary Digital Literacy curricula, redefining what it means for young people to thrive in an Artificial Intelligence (AI)-powered world. Designed for learners aged 5-14, the updated Cambridge Primary Digital Literacy and Lower Secondary Digital Literacy go beyond teaching children how to use technology. They foster digital maturity, equipping students with the judgement, critical thinking, and confidence to decide why, when, and how technology should be used. The refreshed curricula provide schools with a clear, future-facing framework that empowers learners to retain agency in a rapidly evolving digital landscape. New content ranges from online wellbeing to prioritising human intelligence in the age of AI while exploring the challenges and the value that AI can bring to their lives and education. Focus shifts from mastering individual tools to building adaptable, transferable skills to enable learners not only to navigate misinformation and online harms, but to actively shape the technologies influencing their education and their futures. The updated curriculum explores how AI can support multimodal communication across different audiences and platforms, and how structured dialogue with AI systems can deepen research, inquiry and critical thinking. At the same time, it strengthens media literacy and reinforces students’ ownership and understanding of their work. New and updated topics in the curricula include: Curriculum author Beverly Clarke MBE said: “It is essential that educators across all subject areas support young learners to think deeply and critically about AI and their relationship to it. This revised Cambridge curriculum supports schools to engage with AI in a positive, proactive, and informed way, embedding age-appropriate content that empowers learners to develop confidence, curiosity, and critical thinking as they navigate an increasingly AI-enabled world.” Savremena osnovna škola, Belgrade, Serbia Digital Literacy Teacher Nadja Djordjevic was given a first look at the refreshed Lower Secondary curriculum. She said: “I especially appreciate the inclusion of risks such as increased plagiarism due to AI development, and the emphasis on personal ethics for both creators and AI engineers. I see this value as crucial within the creative world – it helps position AI as a supportive tool and at the same time preserve human authenticity and expression.” Schools can teach Digital Literacy as a separate subject or embed the content within other subjects, depending on approach and timetabling. For example, schools could compare the value of thinking and working mathematically against cognitive offloading to AI. There is no formal assessment of the curricula. Learners receive formative feedback through discussion and observation. It is vital that learners demonstrate ownership and understanding of the work that they produce, particularly where AI has been used. title: Sunshine Holdings advances Group-wide digital transformation with new people management platform
body: Saturday Mar 21, 2026 Dailymirror Sunday Times Mirror Edu Tamil Mirror Lankadeepa Middleast Lankadeepa Ada Deshaya Life Online Hi Online Hitad Timesjobs E-Paper Home delivery Advertise with us Print Ads WNL Home Friday, 13 March 2026 00:04 - - {{hitsCtrl.values.hits}} Diversified conglomerate Sunshine Holdings PLC has announced the launch of its new Group-wide people management platform, marking an important milestone in the organisation’s broader digital transformation and governance journey. The platform was unveiled at a special ceremony held in Colombo recently, attended by the Group’s senior leadership including Group CEO Shyam Sathasivam, project teams, Sunshine employees, and technology partners. The initial rollout immediately supports more than 1,700 employees across the Healthcare and Consumer sectors, with further expansion planned across other business units in subsequent phases. Designed to support Sunshine Holdings’ continued growth and operational scale, the new platform establishes a unified digital environment that strengthens workforce governance, enhances organisational visibility, and supports more informed decision-making across the Group. Sathasivam said: “As Sunshine enters its next phase of growth, our ability to scale will depend on how effectively we engage, enable, and develop our people. This initiative represents a foundational investment in creating a more transparent, performance-focused organisation, ensuring that individual growth and organisational progress move forward together. When we strengthen how our people connect their efforts to the Group’s ambitions, we strengthen the future of the entire organisation.” As part of its transformation journey, Sunshine Holdings selected Oracle Human Capital Management (HCM) as the technology foundation for the platform following a structured evaluation process. The cloud-based solution provides a secure and scalable environment that supports enterprise-wide workforce governance, advanced workforce analytics, and stronger organisational planning capabilities. Sunshine Holdings Group Chief People and Corporate Communications Officer Michelle Senanayake said: “Sunshine Holdings is now at an important juncture where it has grown in both scale and complexity. In such a context, it is imperative to ensure that our internal systems evolve in step with our people and our businesses. This platform strengthens the foundations that support our workforce, enabling more meaningful engagement, greater organisational consistency, and an employee experience that reflects the direction in which the Group is moving.” The initiative was implemented through close collaboration between the Group’s HR, digital, and business teams together with its implementation partners, ensuring that global best practices were aligned with local operational needs and organisational culture. Sunshine Holdings Group Chief Digital Officer Infiyaz Ali said: “Sustainable transformation begins with ownership, collaboration, and a willingness to improve the way we work before introducing technology. This initiative reflects a collective commitment across teams to build a strong and disciplined digital foundation, one that will continue to support our people and enable future transformation across the Group.” The new platform strengthens organisational governance, improves operational productivity, and enhances workforce engagement, reinforcing Sunshine Holdings’ long-term vision of building a digitally enabled, people-focused organisation capable of supporting its next phase of growth. title: Embedding human rights, equity and integrity into business leadership
body: Saturday Mar 21, 2026 Dailymirror Sunday Times Mirror Edu Tamil Mirror Lankadeepa Middleast Lankadeepa Ada Deshaya Life Online Hi Online Hitad Timesjobs E-Paper Home delivery Advertise with us Print Ads WNL Home Friday, 13 March 2026 00:02 - - {{hitsCtrl.values.hits}} UN Global Compact Network Sri Lanka Executive Director Rathika de Silva An overview of the session attendees At its 2026 Social Sustainability Program Kick-Off, the UN Global Compact Network Sri Lanka convened business leaders to advance the translation of global ambition into practical corporate action on inclusion, integrity and human rights. On 24 February 2026, the UN Global Compact Network Sri Lanka (Network Sri Lanka) convened business leaders at Barefoot Garden Café for its 2026 Social Sustainability Program Kick-Off, delivered in collaboration with Good Life X. The gathering did more than introduce a calendar of events. It positioned Sri Lanka’s corporate community within the broader direction of the UN Global Compact’s 2026–2030 global strategy — a strategy anchored in three imperatives: equipping companies to act, catalyzing collective action, and advancing the business case for responsible leadership. Equipping companies to act At its core, the 2026 Social Sustainability agenda is designed to move companies from commitment to capability. Within the Diversity & Inclusion Working Group, this means building practical pathways toward equal pay for equal work and strengthening male allyship as a governance issue rather than a cultural afterthought. It means examining sexual and reproductive health, disability inclusion, and mental health not as employee benefits, but as structural determinants of productivity and retention. It means sharpening strategic communications so inclusion is embedded in brand integrity. It also means applying science-based behavioural change approaches to shift organizational culture in measurable ways. Across the Business & Human Rights Working Group, equipping companies takes the form of deepened engagement on decent work and living wage implementation, strengthening human rights due diligence processes, and addressing emerging risk areas such as AI and digital rights. It extends to reinforcing business integrity and anti-corruption frameworks, understanding the social dimensions of a just transition, and recognizing the link between child rights, nutrition, and workforce productivity. The emphasis is practical: tools, dialogue, peer learning, and structured engagement that translate global principles into operational reality. Catalysing collective action The Kick-Off reinforced the Network’s role as a convening platform. Social sustainability challenges — from wage equity to digital governance — cannot be addressed in isolation. They require shared learning, alignment across sectors, and credible collaboration between business and institutional partners. By bringing companies together in an open, conversational format, the session reflected a deliberate move toward collective problem-solving. The choice of Barefoot Garden Café signaled a setting designed for exchange rather than ceremony — reinforcing that responsible business leadership is strengthened through dialogue and shared accountability. Advancing the business case Throughout the evening, one message remained consistent: social sustainability is not CSR: In a global environment defined by regulatory evolution and market scrutiny, responsible business conduct is increasingly synonymous with competitive positioning. The 2026 agenda therefore places equal emphasis on values and value creation — demonstrating that integrating human rights, equity, and integrity into decision-making enhances long-term performance. The session concluded with an interactive quiz facilitated by Good Life X — energetic, collaborative, and reflective of the Network’s engagement model. Essential themes were explored in a format that encouraged connection, reinforcing that strategic conversations can be rigorous without being rigid. As the UN Global Compact advances its 2026–2030 ambition globally, the direction in Sri Lanka is clear: equip companies with the tools to act, catalyze collective momentum, and articulate — with clarity and evidence — the business case for social sustainability. Through five thematic Working Groups — Diversity & Inclusion, Business & Human Rights, Climate Emergency Task Force, Water & Ocean Stewardship, and Sustainable Supply Chain & SME — UN Global Compact Network Sri Lanka convenes the private sector to move from commitment to implementation. These platforms translate global standards into practical action, strengthen governance and accountability, and drive collective progress towards inclusive, resilient, and sustainable economic growth aligned with national and global priorities. title: Elephant House Ice Cream launches new flavours and formats with extrusion technology
body: Saturday Mar 21, 2026 Dailymirror Sunday Times Mirror Edu Tamil Mirror Lankadeepa Middleast Lankadeepa Ada Deshaya Life Online Hi Online Hitad Timesjobs E-Paper Home delivery Advertise with us Print Ads WNL Home Friday, 13 March 2026 00:06 - - {{hitsCtrl.values.hits}} Elephant House Ice Cream has unveiled a new generation of products under its Elephant House Wonder and IMORICH brands, powered by advanced extrusion technology. In a statement, the company said this landmark investment by Ceylon Cold Stores PLC (CCS) strengthens its manufacturing capabilities while setting a new benchmark for texture, structure, and indulgence in the local ice cream category. Installed at CCS’s state-of-the-art manufacturing facility at the BOI Zone in Avissawella, the new extrusion line enables precision layering, indulgent cores, rich inclusions, defined shapes, and premium chocolate coatings at scale. The technology unlocks innovative formats including cakes, logs, and frozen sandwiches, delivering a multi-textured, global-standard experience comparable to leading international brands. For decades, Elephant House Ice Cream has shaped Sri Lanka’s ice cream industry through continuous investment in quality and innovation. While Elephant House Wonder has driven category growth by making joyful indulgence accessible to households nationwide, IMORICH was introduced to meet rising aspirations for premium, world-class experiences. The introduction of extrusion technology marks the next chapter in strengthening both portfolios and future-proofing product development. John Keells Consumer Foods Sector President Daminda Gamlath said: “This investment reflects our long-term commitment to elevating Sri Lanka’s manufacturing excellence and delivering international-quality experiences to local consumers. Through continuous innovation and technology adoption, we aim to lead the evolution of the ice cream category while strengthening our competitiveness in both mass and premium segments.” With further portfolio expansions and bold flavour innovations already in the pipeline, Elephant House Ice Cream continues to shape the future of indulgence in Sri Lanka. title: Ramadan feast awaits at ITC Ratnadipa till 19 March
body: Saturday Mar 21, 2026 Dailymirror Sunday Times Mirror Edu Tamil Mirror Lankadeepa Middleast Lankadeepa Ada Deshaya Life Online Hi Online Hitad Timesjobs E-Paper Home delivery Advertise with us Print Ads WNL Home Friday, 13 March 2026 00:04 - - {{hitsCtrl.values.hits}} This Ramadan, ITC Ratnadipa invites guests to celebrate the holy month with ‘A Feast for the Senses,’ a lavish Iftar buffet at the Indian Ocean Pavilion, available from 18 February to 19 March between 6:00 p.m. and 11:00 p.m. each evening, at LKR 9,500 nett per person. This specially curated dining experience offers an elevated setting for families, friends and colleagues to break their fast together and reflect on the spirit of the season. The spread reflects the generosity associated with Ramadan dining, beginning with traditional kanji in both mutton and chicken, accompanied by dates and mixed nuts to break the fast. Guests can also enjoy six varieties of freshly prepared fried items featuring chicken and mutton, along with shawarma. The buffet further features fragrant biryanis, slow-cooked curries, freshly baked breads, and a selection of savoury delicacies. To conclude the meal, diners can choose from a range of traditional Arabic sweets, desserts, and refreshing beverages, offering a fitting end to the evening’s meal. Alongside these Ramadan specialties, guests can also enjoy an international buffet at the Indian Ocean Pavilion. At the Indian Ocean Pavilion, subtle lantern lighting, refined interiors, and attentive service create an atmosphere that is both elegant and welcoming, providing the perfect backdrop for reflection, celebration, and connection during the holy month. The Indian Ocean Pavilion offers a variety of dining experiences, from all-day dining and brunch to buffets and casual meals, catering to families, large groups, and intimate gatherings alike. Guests can enjoy dining with an ocean view, live cooking stations, open kitchens, and quick bites, ensuring every visit is as much about the experience as it is about the food.
